result: I think about the way dust gathers on a windowsill—how it seems to bloom there, softly, overnight, like bread left too long in cool air. We think of stillness as the absence of motion, but nothing is ever truly still. Particles drift, heat bounces, eyelids flutter in sleep. I like imagining the slow migration of atoms along the painted wood, each mote a traveler with no map, only bright slivers of sun to steer by. Time leaks sideways here: five minutes on this sill equals whole generations in ant-thought, and yet each speck probably contains stars, or at least what’s left of them when they’ve been forgotten by telescopes. Sometimes I wipe it away; sometimes I just watch, letting the grey blanket thicken, wondering if one day it will rise up and declare sovereignty—or simply remember me kindly in its yearly census. Once, I found a beetle upside-down beneath that dust; its legs twitched like the second hand of a dying clock. I turned it over, and it limped away, carrying perhaps an entire micro-cosmos on its bronze wing-covers. The sill felt suddenly larger than the house. I realized then that a border can shrink: insects, galaxies, dandruff, and hope all meet at the same smudged ledge, greeting each other in a language that sounds like a sigh when you lean close enough.
